Reactive Apps

... without frameworks

Emiliano Pisu

Father of 2 lovely "monsters"

D&D master, player ... whatever

Senior Design Engineer, Sensei & Co-Host
@ Dev Dojo IT

linkedin.com/in/pixu1980
github.com/pixu1980

linkedin.com/company/dev-dojo-it/
youtube.com/@devdojo_it
twitch.tv/devdojo_it
@devdojo_it

Meme and
Bold Opinions time

What about frameworks?

Frameworks were great, but they are not the only way to build reactive apps...

...specially with the recent and upcoming evolution of Web APIs combined with LLMs.

🚨 Bold Opinion Alert #1 🚨

After years of "experimentation" in Libraries and Frameworks... the web standards are
coming back into the field!


What about the Web APIs?

A custom implementation of a reactive runtime built on top of modern Web APIs and TC39 Proposals...

...without shipping anything else than vanillaJS runtime code:

  • TC39 Signals proposal for reactive behaviors
  • Proxy API for path-aware state management
  • DOM Parts proposals for persistent update slots
  • Tagged Template Literals for declarative views

🚨 Bold Opinion Alert #2 🚨

With these APIs, we can build real apps with no framework runtime at all... and still have a great DX!


What about the demo?

A Todo List app with: categories, priorities, persistence, filters, bulk actions and a live event log to show the inner workings of the runtime and UI.

🚨 Bold Opinion Alert #3 🚨

If you can build a real demo with these APIs, then you can build real apps... and if you can build real apps, then you can build any app!


Are you nuts?
without frameworks?

The pitch

  • Build a reactive UI stack from platform primitives, not from framework abstractions
  • Use signals and DOM parts to target precise DOM updates instead of diffing the whole tree
  • Keep view authoring expressive with tagged templates, directives and keyed repeat
  • Ship a Todo demo with persistence, filters, bulk actions and a live event log

Why now?

  • TC39 Signals are converging on shared graph semantics for state, computed values and effects
  • DOM Parts are turning framework update slots into an explicit browser-level conversation
  • Tagged templates already give us static structure plus dynamic insertion points today
  • Proxy + CustomEvent make state inspection and tooling straightforward

The browser is catching up

The platform is getting closer to the primitives UI frameworks have been emulating for years. The point is not "frameworks bad", it is understanding how much of the stack can move back into the browser.

Not a final standard, but a very interesting direction

  • TC39 Signals are still Stage 1, so this talk uses a home-grown runtime, not a standards API
  • DOM Parts are still a proposal, so we emulate them with comment anchors and ranges
  • We will map each claim to a concrete proposal or MDN reference, not just to vibes
  • Key takeaway: the platform direction is coherent enough to prototype against it today

Scope and honesty

  • This is not a framework replacement manifesto
  • This is a deliberately small runtime to study data flow, rendering and tradeoffs
  • Some parts are fine-grained, some parts are intentionally coarse and explicit
  • We will look at the actual code, the actual standalone demo and the actual bugs

_clone.js: the store's washing machine for values

Before the store keeps data, emits events, or returns snapshots, it runs values through one careful cloning pipeline. This file exists so the outside world never gets to hold the store's raw internals by accident.
  • getRawValue() unwraps proxies through __raw, so cloning works on the actual target object instead of proxy wrappers
  • clonePlainValue() uses a WeakMap named seen to preserve identity across repeated references and avoid infinite loops
  • It explicitly preserves Date, RegExp, Map, Set, arrays, and plain enumerable object fields
  • deepClone() is just the friendly public wrapper the rest of the store calls when it needs an immutable snapshot
 

function getRawValue(value) {
  if (!isObject(value)) return value
  return value.__raw ?? value
}

export function clonePlainValue(value, seen = new WeakMap()) {
  const raw = getRawValue(value)
  if (!isObject(raw)) return raw
  if (seen.has(raw)) return seen.get(raw)

  if (raw instanceof Date) return new Date(raw.getTime())
  if (raw instanceof RegExp) return new RegExp(raw.source, raw.flags)
  if (raw instanceof Map) {
    const next = new Map()
    seen.set(raw, next)
    for (const [key, entryValue] of raw.entries()) {
      next.set(clonePlainValue(key, seen), clonePlainValue(entryValue, seen))
    }
    return next
  }
}

_guards.js: one tiny question that protects the whole system

This file is almost comically small, and that is exactly why it matters. The store asks this question everywhere: "Is this something I can traverse or proxy, or should I leave it alone?"
  • The guard returns true only for non-null objects, which means primitives stay cheap and untouched
  • The clone layer uses it to stop recursion at strings, numbers, booleans, and null
  • The proxy layer uses it to decide whether a nested value should become another stable proxy
  • This is the kind of boring helper senior engineers love: tiny, reusable, and impossible to misunderstand at 2 AM
 

export function isObject(value) {
  return value !== null && typeof value === "object"
}

_paths.js: translate between human paths and machine paths

The store lets callers say "filters.search", but the proxy engine wants to work one segment at a time. This file is the translator sitting in the middle, quietly making that API feel friendly.
  • toPathArray() accepts strings, arrays, numbers, symbols, or nothing at all, then normalizes everything into one array shape
  • That means get(), set(), and update() can be flexible at the edges but simple internally
  • pathToString() turns the same structure back into dot notation for event payloads like todos.3.done
  • It is small infrastructure, but it keeps the public API pleasant without making the internals messy
 

export function toPathArray(path) {
  if (Array.isArray(path)) return path
  if (path == null || path === "") return []
  return String(path).split(".").filter(Boolean)
}

export function pathToString(path) {
  return toPathArray(path).join(".")
}

_store.js: the actual Proxy store engine

This is where everything comes together. The class owns the raw target tree, creates stable proxies, emits cloned mutation events, and exposes a small API that the rest of the app can reason about without guesswork.
  • The constructor deep-clones the initial state, creates one WeakMap cache for proxy identity, and exposes state as the proxied tree
  • createProxy() intercepts get, set, and deleteProperty, while delegating real object behavior to Reflect
  • emitChange() always clones oldValue and newValue, so listeners receive safe payloads instead of live references into the store
  • get(), set(), update(), replace(), and snapshot() are intentionally tiny wrappers around that proxy graph and event contract
 

createProxy(target, path) {
  if (!isObject(target)) return target
  if (this.proxyCache.has(target)) return this.proxyCache.get(target)

  const proxy = new Proxy(target, {
    get: (raw, key, receiver) => {
      if (key === "__raw") return raw
      if (key === "__path") return path
      const value = Reflect.get(raw, key, receiver)
      if (isObject(value)) return this.createProxy(value, [...path, key])
      return value
    },
    set: (raw, key, value, receiver) => {
      const nextPath = [...path, key]
      const oldValue = raw[key]
      const prepared = clonePlainValue(value)
      const result = Reflect.set(raw, key, prepared, receiver)
      if (oldValue !== prepared) this.emitChange(nextPath, oldValue, prepared)
      return result
    },
  })
}

index.js: one calm public doorway into the store layer

After all that machinery, the public module is intentionally almost boring. That is a feature. Most consumers should not care how cloning, path normalization, or proxy caching work. They should just import Store and move on.
  • This file is a barrel with exactly one export, which keeps imports stable even if internals are later reorganized
  • It is the boundary between "private plumbing" and "public contract"
  • That tiny boundary matters in talks and real products alike, because architecture becomes teachable when entry points are obvious
  • In other words: all the complexity lives behind the curtain, and the consumer gets one friendly button to press
 

/**
 * Public exports for the proxy based store layer.
 */
export { Store } from "./_store.js"

_base-signal.js: the shared wiring for every signal

This file is deliberately tiny. It does not store app data and it does not compute derived values. Its whole job is to say: "I can be tracked, I can be subscribed to, and I can notify listeners when I change."
  • subscribers is a Set, so each listener is stored once and can be removed cleanly later
  • subscribe() adds a callback and returns the tiny unsubscribe function that removes it from the set
  • track() asks the collector context who is currently watching, then registers this signal as a dependency
  • notify() walks a copied array of subscribers, so updates stay safe even if the set changes mid loop
 

export class BaseSignal {
  constructor() {
    this.subscribers = new Set()
    this.__isSignal = true
  }

  subscribe(subscriber) {
    this.subscribers.add(subscriber)
    return () => this.subscribers.delete(subscriber)
  }

  track() {
    const current = getCurrentCollector()
    if (current) current.addDependency(this)
  }

  notify() {
    for (const subscriber of [...this.subscribers]) {
      subscriber()
    }
  }
}

_state-signal.js: the box that actually holds a value

If BaseSignal is the wiring, StateSignal is the first real piece of state. It stores one concrete value, exposes tracked and untracked reads, and only notifies dependents when the value really changes.
  • It extends BaseSignal, so it inherits subscription, tracking, and notification behavior instead of reimplementing them
  • get() calls track() before returning the value, which is how reads become graph edges
  • peek() returns the same value without dependency collection, useful when you want to inspect state quietly
  • set() uses equals as a guard rail, so identical writes do not fan out pointless invalidations
 

export class StateSignal extends BaseSignal {
  constructor(value, options = {}) {
    super()
    this.value = value
    this.equals = options.equals ?? Object.is
  }

  get() {
    this.track()
    return this.value
  }

  peek() {
    return this.value
  }

  set(nextValue) {
    if (this.equals(this.value, nextValue)) return this.value
    this.value = nextValue
    this.notify()
    return this.value
  }
}

_collector-context.js: the clipboard that answers "who is listening right now?"

Reactive tracking looks magical until you reduce it to one simple rule: while a computed or effect runs, remember who it is. Then every signal read can attach itself to that active collector.
  • collectorStack is an array because collectors can nest; the active one always lives on the top
  • getCurrentCollector() is the tiny read primitive used by BaseSignal.track()
  • withCollector() wraps execution in push and pop with try/finally, so cleanup happens even if the callback throws
  • withoutCollector() temporarily silences tracking for the rare cases where a read should not create a dependency
 

const collectorStack = []

export function getCurrentCollector() {
  return collectorStack[collectorStack.length - 1]
}

export function withCollector(collector, callback) {
  pushCollector(collector)
  try {
    return callback()
  } finally {
    popCollector()
  }
}

export function withoutCollector(callback) {
  const current = popCollector()
  try {
    return callback()
  } finally {
    if (current) pushCollector(current)
  }
}

_computed-signal.js: the lazy brain that derives new values

This is the heart of the graph. A computed signal does not own data like state does. Instead, it remembers how to derive data, which signals it depended on last time, and whether that cached answer is still trustworthy.
  • The constructor stores the compute function, the equality check, the dependency map, the cached value, and the dirty flags that control recomputation
  • addDependency() subscribes once per upstream signal and keeps the unsubscribe function in a Map
  • evaluate() is lazy: it does nothing until someone calls get() or peek(), then rebuilds the dependency graph from fresh reads
  • invalidate() marks the value stale and notifies downstream subscribers, so effects and other computeds know they must reevaluate later
 

constructor(compute, options = {}) {
  super()
  this.compute = compute
  this.equals = options.equals ?? Object.is
  this.dependencies = new Map()
  this.cached = undefined
  this.dirty = true
  this.recomputing = false
  this.boundInvalidate = this.invalidate.bind(this)
}

addDependency(signal) {
  if (this.dependencies.has(signal)) return
  const unsubscribe = signal.subscribe(this.boundInvalidate)
  this.dependencies.set(signal, unsubscribe)
}

evaluate() {
  if (!this.dirty) return this.cached
  if (this.recomputing) return this.cached
  this.recomputing = true
  this.cleanupDependencies()
  try {
    const nextValue = withCollector(this, () => this.compute())
    if (this.dirty || !this.equals(this.cached, nextValue)) {
      this.cached = nextValue
    }
    this.dirty = false
    return this.cached
  } finally {
    this.recomputing = false
  }
}

_effect-collector.js: the runner that lets the graph touch the outside world

Effects are where reactivity becomes visible. Rendering, logging, syncing, measuring: none of those produce a derived value, so they live in a collector that tracks reads, resubscribes on every run, and hands reruns to the scheduler.
  • The constructor initializes the callback, dependency map, and active flag, then immediately runs once to discover the first dependency graph
  • addDependency() subscribes to each signal with () => schedule(this), which means invalidations are batched instead of running effects synchronously
  • run() first cleans stale subscriptions, then executes the callback inside withCollector(this, ...) so fresh reads rebuild the graph
  • stop() is the disposer: it flips active off and unsubscribes from everything
 

export class EffectCollector {
  constructor(callback) {
    this.callback = callback
    this.dependencies = new Map()
    this.active = true
    this.run = this.run.bind(this)
    this.run()
  }

  addDependency(signal) {
    if (this.dependencies.has(signal)) return
    const unsubscribe = signal.subscribe(() => schedule(this))
    this.dependencies.set(signal, unsubscribe)
  }

  run() {
    if (!this.active) return
    this.cleanup()
    withCollector(this, () => {
      this.callback()
    })
  }
}

_scheduler.js: the tiny batching loop that keeps rerenders calm

Without scheduling, three quick writes could mean three immediate reruns. This file is the polite traffic controller: collect jobs in a set, wait until the current JavaScript stack is done, then flush them once in a predictable batch.
  • scheduled is a Set, so the same effect can be invalidated many times but only appear once in the queue
  • flushing prevents stacking multiple queueMicrotask() calls for the same flush cycle
  • The microtask waits until the current call stack finishes, which is just enough batching for a tiny runtime like this
  • The while loop matters: if one effect schedules more work during the flush, the scheduler drains that work before finishing
 

const scheduled = new Set()
let flushing = false

export function schedule(effect) {
  scheduled.add(effect)
  if (flushing) return
  flushing = true

  queueMicrotask(() => {
    try {
      while (scheduled.size > 0) {
        const batch = [...scheduled]
        scheduled.clear()
        for (const job of batch) {
          job.run()
        }
      }
    } finally {
      flushing = false
    }
  })
}

index.js: the small public surface the rest of the app actually imports

Internal files are easier to teach when they stay tiny and focused. The outside world, though, wants one friendly door. This module assembles that door: constructors, one escape hatch for untracked reads, one type guard, and one helper to create effects.
  • Signal.State and Signal.Computed expose the two main reactive primitives without leaking file-level implementation details
  • Signal.subtle.untrack intentionally sounds advanced, because turning tracking off is powerful and should feel explicit
  • isSignalLike() gives the renderer a duck-typing check instead of forcing a brittle instanceof contract
  • effect() creates an EffectCollector and returns the disposer, which makes lifecycle cleanup natural for callers
 

export const Signal = {
  State: StateSignal,
  Computed: ComputedSignal,
  subtle: {
    untrack: withoutCollector,
  },
}

export function isSignalLike(value) {
  return Boolean(
    value &&
    typeof value.get === "function" &&
    value.__isSignal
  )
}

export function effect(callback) {
  const runner = new EffectCollector(callback)
  return () => runner.stop()
}

_template-helpers.js: the tiny IR every template expression becomes

Before the engine can render anything, it needs one honest shape for "a template", and one honest shape for "a directive". This file gives us exactly that, with almost no magic.
  • html(strings, ...values) does not touch the DOM; it returns a plain object with kind, strings, and values
  • directive(name, payload) brands directive results with a private Symbol, so parts can recognize them without stringly typed hacks
  • isDirective() checks that brand and can optionally narrow by name, which is how parts distinguish model from repeat
  • model() and repeat() are just friendly wrappers over directive(), so the authoring API stays declarative
 

export function html(strings, ...values) {
  return {
    kind: "template-result",
    strings,
    values,
  }
}

export function directive(name, payload) {
  return {
    [directiveBrand]: true,
    name,
    payload,
  }
}

export function model(config) {
  return directive("model", config)
}

export function repeat(items, key, renderItem) {
  return directive("repeat", { items, key, renderItem })
}

_part.js: one tiny base class for reactive DOM slots

A lot of the engine becomes easier to understand once you see this file: a DOM part is just a place that can hold a value, optionally subscribe to a signal, and clean itself up when the source changes.
  • value stores the last committed input, which gives concrete parts a shared place to remember what they rendered
  • signalCleanup stores the unsubscribe function for the currently bound signal, so replacing a binding does not leak subscriptions
  • bindSignal() subscribes once, then immediately pushes signal.get() through the same callback path used for later updates
  • disposeSignal() is boring on purpose: one cleanup path, reused by attribute, property, and child parts
 

export class Part {
  constructor() {
    this.value = undefined
    this.signalCleanup = null
  }

  bindSignal(signal, callback) {
    this.disposeSignal()
    this.signalCleanup = signal.subscribe(() => {
      callback(signal.get())
    })
    callback(signal.get())
  }

  disposeSignal() {
    if (this.signalCleanup) this.signalCleanup()
    this.signalCleanup = null
  }
}

_event-part.js: the whole event system in one very small file

This slide is a nice reminder that not every runtime primitive needs to be clever. Event bindings are not tracked values. They are simply listener replacement with disciplined cleanup.
  • EventPart does not extend Part, because event listeners are not signal subscriptions and do not share that lifecycle
  • setValue() always removes the old listener first, so rerenders can safely replace closures without stacking handlers
  • Non function values are ignored, which keeps bad bindings harmless instead of half working
  • The class stores exactly one active listener reference, because removal in the DOM API must use the same function identity
 

export class EventPart {
  constructor(element, name) {
    this.element = element
    this.name = name
    this.listener = null
  }

  setValue(value) {
    if (this.listener) {
      this.element.removeEventListener(this.name, this.listener)
      this.listener = null
    }
    if (typeof value !== "function") return
    this.listener = value
    this.element.addEventListener(this.name, this.listener)
  }
}

_property-part.js: write to DOM properties, not HTML text

Attributes are strings on markup. Properties are live JavaScript fields on elements. This part exists for the moments when the engine must talk to the actual DOM object, not to serialized HTML.
  • It extends Part, so property bindings automatically gain the same signal subscription and cleanup behavior as the other reactive parts
  • setValue() branches only once: if the input is signal like, subscribe and resolve it; otherwise commit the raw value
  • commit() writes straight to this.element[this.name], which is why booleans, objects, and live form values work correctly
  • The file is intentionally tiny because all the complexity belongs in the DOM, not in the wrapper around it
 

export class PropertyPart extends Part {
  constructor(element, name) {
    super()
    this.element = element
    this.name = name
  }

  setValue(value) {
    if (isSignalLike(value)) {
      this.bindSignal(value, (resolved) => this.commit(resolved))
      return
    }
    this.disposeSignal()
    this.commit(value)
  }

  commit(value) {
    this.element[this.name] = value
  }
}

_attribute-part.js: plain attributes plus the most opinionated directive in the engine

This file starts simple and then becomes surprisingly sophisticated. It can write ordinary attributes, subscribe to signals, and run the two way model() directive with cursor preservation and a select specific bug fix.
  • setValue() is the traffic cop: route model(...) to commitModel(), route signals through bindSignal(), otherwise commit a normal attribute
  • commit() maps null and false to attribute removal, true to an empty attribute, and everything else to a string
  • commitModel() caches bindings by event, property, and signal identity, so stable model shapes do not tear down listeners every rerender
  • sync() handles checked versus value, preserves text selection while typing, and syncAfterRender() gives <select> one extra microtask so options exist first
 

setValue(value) {
  if (this.name === "model" && isDirective(value, "model")) {
    this.commitModel(value.payload)
    this.value = value
    return
  }

  if (isSignalLike(value)) {
    this.disposeModel()
    this.bindSignal(value, (resolved) => this.commit(resolved))
    return
  }

  this.disposeModel()
  this.disposeSignal()
  this.commit(value)
}

const syncAfterRender = () => {
  sync()
  if (this.element instanceof HTMLSelectElement) {
    queueMicrotask(() => {
      if (this._modelBinding === binding && this.element.isConnected) sync()
    })
  }
}

_range.js: comment markers become a real DOM editing toolkit

Proposed DOM Parts would give the browser native update slots. We do not have that here, so this file builds the minimum surgery kit needed to treat two comments as a movable, replaceable DOM range.
  • clearRange() removes everything between start and end markers without touching the markers themselves
  • moveRangeBefore() lifts a whole block into a fragment and reinserts it, so keyed list moves can preserve real DOM nodes instead of recreating them
  • isRangeBeforeReference() prevents pointless moves during repeat reconciliation by checking whether the block is already in the right place
  • normalizeNode(), isIterable(), and inferModelProperty() are the tiny helpers that let the bigger parts stay readable
 

export function clearRange(start, end) {
  let current = start.nextSibling
  while (current && current !== end) {
    const next = current.nextSibling
    current.remove()
    current = next
  }
}

export function moveRangeBefore(start, end, referenceNode) {
  const fragment = document.createDocumentFragment()
  let current = start
  while (current) {
    const next = current.nextSibling
    fragment.append(current)
    if (current === end) break
    current = next
  }
  referenceNode.parentNode.insertBefore(fragment, referenceNode)
}

_child-node-part.js: the part that can render almost anything

If AttributePart speaks to element fields, ChildNodePart speaks to the area between two markers. Text, nodes, nested templates, iterables, keyed repeat, signal driven lists: this is the heavyweight operator of the engine.
  • setValue() resolves signals first, so child content can stay reactive without every caller writing manual subscriptions
  • commit() branches across repeat directives, template results, iterables, and single values, which is why one placeholder can accept many shapes
  • commitTemplate() reuses the existing template instance when the template literal identity is the same, updating values in place instead of rebuilding DOM
  • commitRepeat() keeps a keyed map of blocks, walks backwards to get a stable anchor, moves ranges instead of nodes one by one, and can subscribe to signal driven item sources
 

function createBlock(itemKey, referenceNode) {
  const start = document.createComment(`repeat-start:${itemKey}`)
  const end = document.createComment(`repeat-end:${itemKey}`)
  referenceNode.parentNode.insertBefore(start, referenceNode)
  referenceNode.parentNode.insertBefore(end, referenceNode)
  return { key: itemKey, start, end, part: new ChildNodePart(start, end) }
}

commitRepeat({ items, key, renderItem }) {
  const source = isSignalLike(items) ? items.get() : items
  const list = Array.isArray(source) ? source : isIterable(source) ? [...source] : []
  const state = this.repeatState ?? { blocks: new Map() }
  const nextBlocks = new Map()
  let referenceNode = this.end

  for (let index = list.length - 1; index >= 0; index -= 1) {
    const item = list[index]
    const itemKey = key(item)
    let block = state.blocks.get(itemKey) ?? createBlock(itemKey, referenceNode)
    if (block.item !== item) block.part.setValue(renderItem(item))
    nextBlocks.set(itemKey, block)
    referenceNode = block.start
  }
}

_parts.js: one import seam for all DOM part implementations

This file is short, but it improves the architecture in a very practical way. The template instance builder can depend on one barrel instead of juggling a handful of sibling imports every time it maps descriptors to concrete parts.
  • It reexports AttributePart, ChildNodePart, EventPart, Part, and PropertyPart from one place
  • That gives _template-instance.js a single dependency edge for all concrete part types
  • Barrels like this are not about saving keystrokes; they are about making module boundaries legible
  • In a talk, this matters because people can understand the graph at a glance instead of parsing import noise
 

export { AttributePart } from "./_attribute-part.js"
export { ChildNodePart } from "./_child-node-part.js"
export { EventPart } from "./_event-part.js"
export { Part } from "./_part.js"
export { PropertyPart } from "./_property-part.js"

_template-instance-ref.js: a tiny registry that breaks a circular import cleanly

This is one of those files that makes seasoned engineers smile. Instead of pretending circular imports do not exist, the runtime isolates the cycle behind a tiny register and read API with one loud failure mode.
  • TemplateInstance lives in module scope as a mutable slot, not as a direct import
  • setTemplateInstanceClass() registers the concrete class exactly once during template engine setup
  • getTemplateInstanceClass() lets ChildNodePart instantiate nested templates without importing the class directly
  • If setup did not happen, the helper throws immediately, which is much better than silently rendering broken DOM
 

let TemplateInstance

export function setTemplateInstanceClass(cls) {
  TemplateInstance = cls
}

export function getTemplateInstanceClass() {
  if (!TemplateInstance) {
    throw new Error("TemplateInstance class not registered.")
  }

  return TemplateInstance
}

_template-instance.js: compile once, clone fast, bind precise parts

This is the parser and the materializer. It turns template literals into cached descriptors, then turns those descriptors into concrete part objects inside a cloned fragment. If the engine feels magical anywhere, it is here.
  • getTemplate() builds synthetic markup with placeholders, parses it once, walks the fragment, and stores descriptor metadata in a WeakMap keyed by the original TemplateStringsArray
  • Descriptors record type, index, name, raw attribute name, and node path, so later instances can find the exact DOM spot without reparsing strings
  • The constructor clones the cached template fragment, resolves descriptor paths, and swaps placeholders for ChildNodePart, AttributePart, PropertyPart, or EventPart
  • update(values) is then almost boring: walk the values array and push each value into its matching part
 

function getTemplate(strings) {
  let record = templateCache.get(strings)
  if (record) return record

  let markup = ""
  for (let index = 0; index < strings.length - 1; index += 1) {
    const chunk = strings[index]
    markup += chunk
    markup += chunk.match(ATTRIBUTE_PART_RE)
      ? `__part_${index}__`
      : ``
  }

  const template = document.createElement("template")
  template.innerHTML = markup + strings[strings.length - 1]
  record = { template, descriptors }
  templateCache.set(strings, record)
  return record
}

update(values) {
  for (let index = 0; index < values.length; index += 1) {
    const part = this.parts.get(index)
    if (part) part.setValue(values[index])
  }
}

_template.js: the composition root that turns helpers and instances into rendering

This file is where the engine becomes usable. It wires the concrete TemplateInstance class into the registry, reexports the helper surface, and gives us a render() function that keeps one stable root part per container.
  • setTemplateInstanceClass(TemplateInstance) happens once at module load, which closes the loop opened by _template-instance-ref.js
  • The file reexports directive, html, isDirective, model, and repeat, so consumers do not have to know about the helpers module directly
  • render() creates one pair of root comments and one root ChildNodePart the first time a container is used
  • Every later render reuses that root part, which means the container itself stays stable and only the controlled range changes
 

setTemplateInstanceClass(TemplateInstance)

export function render(result, container) {
  let rootPart = container.__rootPart
  if (!rootPart) {
    const start = document.createComment("root:start")
    const end = document.createComment("root:end")
    container.textContent = ""
    container.append(start, end)
    rootPart = new ChildNodePart(start, end)
    container.__rootPart = rootPart
  }
  rootPart.setValue(result)
}

index.js: the single friendly import path the rest of the app sees

After all that plumbing, the public entry is intentionally boring. That is the mark of a good boundary. Internals can be rich, but the caller should get one calm door and a small set of names.
  • This module reexports directive, html, isDirective, model, render, and repeat from one place
  • That means views and components never need to know whether the engine is split across 2 files or 20
  • In architecture terms, this is the explicit public contract of the whole template layer
  • In conference terms, this is the slide where you can finally say: "all that machinery collapses into one import"
 

export {
  directive,
  html,
  isDirective,
  model,
  render,
  repeat,
} from "./_template.js"

components/App/_App.js: one real app shell, not one lonely button

This chapter is where the tiny runtime stops being theory. The app view is a little house made of small rooms, and every room uses the same store, signals, and template helpers we already studied.
  • appView() assembles the header, stats, list, side panels, debug panel, and both modals
  • Every room is just a function that returns html, so composition stays plain and readable
  • No room owns secret state; they all read from the same shared truth box
  • That is why this demo feels like a small product slice, not a stage prop
 

export function appView() {
  return html`
    ${appHeader()}
    

      ${statsRow()}
      ${todoListPanel()}
    

    
${filtersPanel()} ${bulkActionsPanel()}

    
${debugPanel()}

    ${todoModal()} ${categoryModal()}
  `
}

data/_data.js: the toy box where every piece starts

Before the store can react, it needs one complete starting picture. This file builds that picture in one place, so reset, persistence, and rendering all agree on the same state shape.
  • todos, categories, and draft hold the work the user can create and edit
  • filters and preferences are also state, because the app must remember how to look and what to show
  • debug and ui prove that temporary interface details still belong in the same tree
  • createSeedData() is reused by first load and by resetDemo(), so the app can always grow back from one seed
 

export function createSeedData() {
  return {
    todos: [/* starter todos */],
    categories: ["Inbox", "Talk", "Engine", "Assets", "Research"],
    draft: {
      title: "",
      notes: "",
      category: "Inbox",
      priority: "medium",
      dueDate: new Date(now + ONE_DAY_MS).toISOString().slice(0, 10),
    },
    filters: { search: "", category: "all", status: "all", priority: "all", sortBy: "createdAt", sortDir: "desc" },
    debug: { paused: false, logs: [] },
    preferences: { colorScheme: "system", theme: "studio", language: "en" },
    ui: {
      categoryModal: { open: false, value: "", error: "" },
      todoModal: { open: false, error: "" },
    },
  }
}

state/_store-setup.js: the bell that says "something changed"

The proxy store already knows when data changes. This file turns that fact into app behavior: save the snapshot, update the debug log, and ring one tiny signal so the rest of the UI can wake up.
  • store is the shared truth box created from the seed state
  • tickState is a tiny signal used as a redraw bell, and equals: () => false means every ring counts
  • handleStoreChange() appends a debug entry, persists a snapshot, then bumps tickState
  • This is the practical bridge between the store chapter and the signals chapter
 

export const store = new Store(initialState)

export const tickState = new Signal.State(0, {
  equals: () => false,
})

function handleStoreChange(event) {
  if (!store.state.debug.paused && event.detail.path !== "debug.logs") {
    appendDebugLog(event.detail)
  }
  persistState()
  tickState.set(performance.now())
}

window.addEventListener("store:change", handleStoreChange)

state/_computed.js: little helpers that keep counting for us

A five year old version is: these are tiny robots. You give them the raw toy pile, and they keep answering questions like "which todos should I show?" and "how many are done?"
  • visibleTodos listens to tickState and runs the whole filtering pipeline
  • summary walks the full list once, then hands ready-made counts to smaller computed signals
  • totalCount, openCount, visibleCount, and friends reuse summary instead of recounting everything
  • Components read clean answers, not raw loops and conditions
 

export const visibleTodos = new Signal.Computed(() => {
  tickState.get()
  return pipelineTodos(
    store.state.todos,
    store.state.filters,
    store.state.preferences.language,
  )
})

export const summary = new Signal.Computed(() => {
  tickState.get()
  const todos = store.state.todos
  let total = 0
  let completed = 0
  let selected = 0
  for (const todo of todos) {
    total += 1
    if (todo.completed) completed += 1
    if (todo.selected) selected += 1
  }
  return { total, completed, open: total - completed, selected, visible: visibleTodos.get().length }
})

data/_pipeline.js: one filter pipe, one job at a time

Instead of making lots of temporary arrays, the app lets todos walk through a pipe. Each filter looks at one todo, decides yes or no, and passes only the matching ones to the next step.
  • fromArray() turns the todo list into a lazy stream
  • filterBySearch(), filterByCategory(), filterByStatus(), and filterByPriority() each do one simple job
  • Only the final sort step materializes the list, so the pipeline stays cheap and easy to inspect
  • visibleTodos simply asks the pipeline for the ready-made answer
 

export function pipelineTodos(todos, filters, language) {
  const iterator = filterByPriority(
    filterByStatus(
      filterByCategory(
        filterBySearch(fromArray(todos), filters.search, language),
        filters.category,
      ),
      filters.status,
    ),
    filters.priority,
  )

  return sortTodos(iterator, filters.sortBy, filters.sortDir, language)
}

state/_actions.js: the hands that move the toys

Actions are the places where the app says "change the world now". They are boring on purpose: find something, replace it, add it, remove it, or rebuild the whole state from the seed.
  • updateTodo() finds one item and replaces it with a patched copy
  • addTodo() turns the shared draft into a new todo, then resets the draft for the next creation
  • deleteSelected(), deleteCompleted(), and friends operate on whole arrays with plain map() or filter()
  • resetDemo() replaces the entire state tree while keeping the current preferences
 

export function updateTodo(id, patch) {
  const index = store.state.todos.findIndex((todo) => todo.id === id)
  if (index < 0) return
  const current = store.state.todos[index]
  store.state.todos[index] = {
    ...current,
    ...patch,
  }
}

export function resetDemo() {
  const { preferences } = store.snapshot()
  store.replace({
    ...createSeedData(),
    preferences: { ...preferences },
  })
  tickState.set(performance.now())
}

src/scripts/index.js: when the bell rings, paint again

This is the slide where the chapters shake hands. Signals watch, the template engine renders, and the DOM changes for real.
  • The first effect() mirrors theme, color scheme, and language onto the document for CSS and accessibility
  • The second effect() watches tickState and calls render(appView(), root)
  • requestAnimationFrame() measures the app shell after the DOM has been updated
  • So the runtime story is: store change, bell rings, effect runs, template engine paints
 

effect(() => {
  tickState.get()
  syncDocumentPreferences()
})

effect(() => {
  tickState.get()
  render(appView(), root)
  scheduleAppShellSizeSync()
})

tickState.set(performance.now())

helpers/_helpers.js: two tiny translators for all our inputs

We do not want every text field and checkbox to remember how to read and write the store. So the app builds two small helpers, and then reuses them everywhere.
  • storeModel(path) is the generic translator for fields like filters.search or preferences.theme
  • todoModel(todoId, field) is the special translator for one field on one todo card
  • Both helpers are just model({ signal, get, set }), which means the same directive powers the whole app
  • This is the practical use of the template engine chapter: tiny reusable bindings instead of manual event code everywhere
 

export function storeModel(path, options = {}) {
  return model({
    signal: tickState,
    get: () => store.get(path),
    set: (value) => store.set(path, value),
    ...options,
  })
}

export function todoModel(todoId, field, options = {}) {
  return model({
    signal: tickState,
    get: () => getTodoById(todoId)?.[field],
    set: (value) => updateTodo(todoId, { [field]: value }),
    ...options,
  })
}

components/AppHeader/_AppHeader.js: big buttons on top, tiny writes underneath

The header looks rich, but its behavior is simple. Buttons call actions, selects use storeModel(), and the rest of the app updates because the same shared store keeps listening.
  • @click=${resetDemo}, @click=${openTodoModal}, and @click=${openCategoryModal} are plain event parts
  • Theme, language, and color scheme selects write straight into preferences.* paths
  • The document dataset effect then mirrors those preferences so CSS and translations can react
  • This proves the runtime is driving the whole shell, not just the list in the middle

components/TodoItem/_TodoItem.js: one card where model() shows its magic

This is the best "look, it is really used" slide. One todo card has checkboxes, text inputs, selects, a textarea, and a delete button, but they all stay tiny because the helpers do the boring work.
  • Checkboxes use prop: "checked" and event: "change" so DOM booleans map cleanly to store booleans
  • Title, notes, category, priority, and due date all reuse the same todoModel() helper
  • The delete button is just @click, so event parts and model parts live together happily
  • The card also mirrors state into data-state and data-priority, which keeps styling honest and declarative

components/TodoList/_TodoList.js: one render function becomes a whole living list

A single todo card is nice, but the real trick is many cards that stay stable when filters, sorting, or language change. That is where repeat() earns its lunch.
  • visibleLabel drops straight into the template as a computed signal, so the summary text updates itself
  • repeat(visibleTodos, key, todoItem) turns one render function into a full ordered list
  • The key uses todo.id and language, so locale changes can rebuild the right text without losing list identity accidentally
  • This is the everyday use of the repeat directive and the range-moving logic from the template engine chapter
 

export function todoListPanel() {
  const language = store.state.preferences.language

  return html`
    

      

        
${t(language, "sections.reactiveList")}

        
${visibleLabel}

      

      
    
        ${repeat(visibleTodos, (todo) => `${todo.id}:${language}`, todoItem)}
      

    

  `
}

StatsRow, Filters, BulkActions, DebugPanel: the same state wearing four costumes

The panels around the list are great teaching material because they all read the same brain in different ways. Some show counts, some write filters, some call actions, and one simply watches every mutation happen.
  • StatsRow prints computed counts, so cards stay dumb and the math stays centralized
  • Filters writes into filters.* paths with storeModel(), which immediately changes visibleTodos
  • BulkActions calls real action functions like selectAllVisible() and deleteSelected()
  • DebugPanel even pauses itself through the same store, then renders the log with another repeat()
 

${statCard(totalCount, t(language, "stats.total"))}







${repeat(debugLogs, (entry) => entry.id, debugLogEntry)}

TodoModal and CategoryModal: popups are just more state

A common beginner mistake is to think dialogs need special magic. Here they do not. They simply open when the store says open, and their fields write into shared state just like the rest of the app.
  • todoModal() reads ui.todoModal.open and returns nothing when the dialog should stay closed
  • The whole form writes into draft.*, so add and edit flows can share one basket of temporary values
  • categoryModal() writes to ui.categoryModal.value and shows validation errors from state
  • Submit handlers call addTodo() or addCategory(), which means modals stay thin and business logic stays in actions
 

if (!modal.open) return ""

The runtime is not trapped in slides. It opens as a real app tab.

No deck-only sandbox. The audience can open the same standalone demo we publish, poke it, and see the exact store, signals, directives, and render loop we just walked through. 

<a
  href="https://pixu1980.github.io/reactive-apps-without-frameworks-demo/"
  target="_blank"
  rel="noreferrer"
>
  Open the standalone demo
</a>

Open the standalone demo ↗

What the demo really proves

  • The store is the truth box, and every part of the app keeps reading from the same box
  • tickState is the bell that wakes computed signals and render effects after each mutation
  • model() and repeat() are not cute helpers; they are the practical fingers that touch inputs and lists all day long
  • A tiny runtime can power a surprisingly rich interface, but the real product question is still how much of that runtime you want to own yourself

Wrapping up

The DOM can move back to the center of the rendering story. Signals, parts and tagged templates are enough to build a reactive app, as long as we stay honest about the missing abstractions and the engineering cost.

Strengths

  • DOM-first rendering, with node reuse instead of virtual tree diffing
  • Small surface area, so the runtime stays readable and debuggable
  • Concrete DX, with signals, directives and expressive templates
  • Observable state, because every write has a path and an event payload

Tradeoffs

  • The standards story is still evolving, so some primitives are emulated
  • The runtime still needs real engineering, not just enthusiasm
  • It is easy to accidentally rebuild framework complexity in a worse form
  • Great as a learning base, not as a universal dogma

Technical lessons from the demo

  • A coarse invalidation bridge can still produce incremental DOM updates if parts stay stable
  • model on <select> needs post-render resync because options materialize after the binding
  • A live event log is only useful if it describes the final persisted state, not an intermediate one
  • Small runtimes make bugs easier to understand because the entire stack still fits in your head

References and status

This deck runs on Reveal.js 6.0.0. The talk borrows ideas from TC39 Signals and the DOM Parts proposal, but the code you saw is an explicit, local implementation built for learning and experimentation.

Vanilla JS is not about giving up DX. It is about understanding which abstractions are essential, which are optional, and how far the platform can already take us.

Personal Links

DevDojo Links

© 1987-2025 - Warner Bros. Entertainment Inc.

Thanks.
